nativeRange.habitat: Seaward rocky reefs and rubble slopes with extensive algal turf, typically between 6 and 30 m depth.

decorPreferences: caves and crevices for grazing and refuge

diet.notes: In the wild, the Rusty Angelfish grazes on filamentous algae, detritus, and small benthic invertebrates. In aquaria it generally accepts spirulina and marine-algae sheets, mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and quality angelfish pellet preparations. Offering a varied, algae-rich diet is widely recommended to help preserve coloration and to reduce the likelihood of coral nipping.

goodWith: most peaceful to semi-aggressive reef fish
avoidWith: other dwarf angelfish (Centropyge spp.) in tanks under ~400 L
very docile species that may be bullied

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Izuzuki diver. Correctly ID'd Centropyge ferrugata: rust-orange lower flank, dark flank speckles, and blue-margined dorsal/anal fins all visible on a live in-situ reef individual. Only 320x240 (thumbnail-resolution) and colours slightly muted, but it is the sole commercial-friendly live photograph available; all higher-res GBIF/iNaturalist copies are CC BY-NC.

summary: Small Western Pacific dwarf angelfish (~10 cm) with rust-red flanks and black flank dots. Generally considered reef-safe with caution; best kept singly in a mature, rock-rich aquarium of around 300 L.
careNotes: The Rusty Angelfish (Centropyge ferrugata) is a dwarf angelfish from the Western Pacific — southern Japan, Taiwan, and the Philippines — typically reaching about 8–10 cm. It is broadly considered hardy among dwarf angelfish, though it does best in a mature aquarium with stable water quality and plenty of live rock for grazing and shelter. It is generally regarded as reef-safe with caution: some individuals have been known to nip at LPS and SPS coral polyps (large-polyp and small-polyp stony corals respectively), soft coral polyps, and clam mantles, particularly when underfed. Keeping the diet varied and algae-rich tends to reduce this risk. The Rusty Angelfish is widely considered highly territorial toward other dwarf angelfish and generally does best as the sole Centropyge in tanks smaller than around 400 L. Like other pygmy angelfishes, this species is a sequential hermaphrodite — females can transition to male — and reportedly can change sex in either direction. Wild populations form loose harems; in captivity a male with one or two smaller females can sometimes coexist in larger, well-rockworked systems.
breedingNotes: The Rusty Angelfish is rarely bred in home aquaria. Like other Centropyge, it is a pelagic spawner, releasing eggs into the water column at dusk. Rearing the free-swimming larvae has been achieved by specialist breeders but is considered an advanced undertaking.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source: adult size, tank size (70 US gal), temperament, diet, reef-safe-with-caution, temperature 72–78 °F, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, care level moderate.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Substituted for the assigned Reef2Reef wiki secondary, which returned HTTP 403. Confirmed family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes, max length 10 cm TL, Western Pacific distribution (Tanabe Bay, southern Japan to SW Taiwan and Philippines), depth 6–30 m, aquarium temperature range 25–29.3 °C, and bi-directional sex change.

additional.name: ReefApp encyclopedia
additional.notes: Third source for cross-verification: 10 cm adult size, 200 L minimum tank, reef-safe with caution, omnivore, protogynous sex change noted, Western Pacific range confirmed.

additional.name: Reeflings species guide
additional.notes: Fourth source: 10 cm size, ~200 L minimum tank, temperature 24–28 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.026, care level moderate-to-easy, territorial toward other dwarf angels in small tanks.

additional.name: Frank Baensch — Pygmy Angelfishes captive care
additional.notes: Specialist source confirming Centropyge are protogynous hermaphrodites and form harems; recommends 75 US gal+ for healthy keeping and notes social disputes in groups housed under 100 US gal. Used to justify biasing minimum volume upward from the retailer figure.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning occurs at dusk, with eggs released into the open water column. Larval rearing is specialist work and is rarely accomplished by hobbyists.
